straight_beat.name: Russ Havelock
straight_beat.column:
The D-backs’ 4-3 win over the Orioles yesterday felt like a footnote in the broader narrative of this season. Not because it wasn’t important—far from it—but because it’s another data point in a season where the team’s trajectory is less about individual games and more about the calculus of consistency. Desert math, as I’ve said before, isn’t about adding up wins and losses; it’s about understanding the margins where a .500 team can still feel like a contender. And that’s what this club is doing.  

The game itself was a microcosm of the team’s recent struggles and flashes of brilliance. A walk-off win, of course, would have been the cherry on top, but the D-backs settled for a close victory, which is exactly what they’ve been doing all year. They’ve won six of their last eight games, but four of those have been by one run. It’s the kind of performance that makes you lean into the standings rather than the scoreboard. You don’t win 10 games in 18 tries by being perfect; you win by being stubbornly good in the moments that matter.  

Which brings me to the bullpen. Manager Torey Lovullo has been

optimist.name: Dolores 'Dolly' Sanderlin
optimist.column:
The D-backs lost yesterday, but let’s not dwell on the scoreboard. A walk-off loss in extras? That’s just the universe setting up a bigger comeback. Keep the pool open, we’re hosting October.  

The real story wasn’t the final out—it was the way the rotation held together. Four innings of solid work from the starters, a few shaky moments, but overall, they kept the game within reach. The bullpen, always the unsung heroes, stepped in when needed. You can’t argue with that kind of grit. The rotation’s got legs, and legs mean something in this league.  

Now, let’s talk about the standings. Three and a half games back? That’s not a chasm—it’s a gap that can be bridged with a little patience and a lot of heart. The NL West is a brutal division, but the D-backs have the tools to keep pace. They’ve got the young arms, the veteran presence, and a lineup that’s starting to find its rhythm. The key is consistency, and consistency

pessimist.name: Jack Durmire
pessimist.column:
The D-backs won again. Four games, three losses, and a bullpen that’s still pretending it’s 2005. Let’s not get carried away. The same script plays out every Tuesday: a starter whoches through five innings, the offense stumbles into a rally, and then the relievers—oh, the relievers—turn it into a soap opera. Yesterday’s game was no different. A four-run sixth inning, a walk-off home run, and a bullpen that looked like it had just finished a three-game series in the minors. Small-market excuses, sure, but even the most optimistic fan can’t ignore the math.  

The bullpen’s been the team’s most persistent ghost since the Kendrick era. You can name every prospect the front office traded in that time, and none of them have ever amounted to anything. They’ve spent the last decade trying to build a rotation out of spare parts and hoping the offense would compensate. It’s a formula that’s worked exactly zero times. Last night’s game was a microcosm: a starter who held the opposition to one run, then a bullpen that allowed two in the sixth, only to rally back with a walk-off. The same movie. Different Tuesday.  

And let’s not pretend the offense is the savior here. They’ve been good enough to win 10 games, sure, but they’re not good enough to win 100. The lineup’s got flashes of brilliance—some guy named Correa, some guy named Martin—but they’re all one injury or one trade away. The front office keeps talking about “building for the future,” but when your best player is a guy who can’t hit a slider, you’re not building
